raw_text:
```json
{
  "claims": [
    "The multi-billion beauty industry in the U.S. continues to profit from selling anti-aging products marketed to children.",
    "Anti-aging products contain chemicals that may be damaging to children’s young, sensitive skin.",
    "There is a growing consensus among experts regarding the harms that anti-aging products can cause to children.",
    "Children as young as eight using anti-aging products could experience irreversible skin problems.",
    "Children have more sensitive skin than adults, making them more vulnerable to external irritants and environmental factors.",
    "Chronic irritation and inflammation can compromise a child's skin barrier, affecting its ability to protect against environmental stressors.",
    "Using harsh active ingredients in anti-aging products could accelerate skin damage in children.",
    "Common ingredients in anti-aging skin products, such as retinol and alpha hydroxy acids, can cause skin irritations and potentially lead to conditions like dermatitis and eczema.",
    "Regulatory bodies like the FDA and EMA do not require testing of anti-aging products on children, as these products are not intended for their use.",
    "There are no clinical studies providing evidence that anti-aging products are safe for children to use.",
    "An 11-year-old reported experiencing skin reactions like burns and bumps after using anti-aging products marketed to her.",
    "The proposed bill AB 728 would prohibit the sale of over-the-counter anti-aging products containing vitamin A derivatives to minors."
  ]
}
```
